questions.question:
Read the following paragraph and choose the correct statement.

Climate change has reduced human security and threatened human well being. An ignored reality of human progress is that human security largely depends upon environmental security. But on the contrary, human progress seems contradictory to environmental security. To keep up with the required level is a challenge to be addressed by one and all. One of the ways to curb the climate change may be suitable scientific innovations, while the other may be the implementation of strict laws.

A. Human progress and security are positively associated with environmental security.
B. Human progress is contradictory to environmental security.
C. Human security is contradictory to environmental security.
D. Human progress depends upon environmental security.
questions.answer: B. Human progress is contradictory to environmental security.
questions.to_find: the correct statement logically supported by the passage about human progress and environmental security

understand.plain: The passage discusses how human progress conflicts with environmental security, even though human security depends on environmental security. Climate change is presented as a direct threat to human well-being. The key tension is that while human security needs environmental security, human progress works against it.

keywords.explain: The condition of a stable, healthy natural environment that supports human life and well-being.
keywords.example: Clean air, safe water, and stable climate are components of environmental security.

keywords.explain: Advancement in technology, economy, and living standards, often at the cost of natural resources.
keywords.example: Industrialization raises GDP but increases carbon emissions, illustrating the contradiction.

keywords.explain: Long-term shifts in global temperature and weather patterns, largely driven by human activities.
keywords.example: Rising sea levels and extreme weather events are consequences of climate change.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is purely text-based passage comprehension.

given.aim: Identify the statement that is directly supported by the passage

terms.term: Human security depends on environmental security
terms.meaning: The passage states this explicitly as an 'ignored reality'.
terms.example: Safe food and water require a healthy environment.
terms.connects: Rules out option C which reverses this relationship.

terms.meaning: The passage explicitly states human progress 'seems contradictory to environmental security'.
terms.example: Deforestation for development reduces environmental security.

terms.meaning: Option A claims human progress is positively linked to environmental security, which the passage explicitly denies.
terms.example: The passage says they are contradictory, not positively associated.

terms.meaning: Option D confuses human SECURITY depending on environment with human PROGRESS depending on it.
terms.example: The passage distinguishes between security (depends on environment) and progress (contradicts environment).
terms.connects: Eliminates option D as a misread of the passage.

given.plan: Check each option against the passage text for direct support or contradiction.

steps.title: Identify key claims in the passage
steps.apply: Passage claims: (1) Climate change reduces human security. (2) Human security depends on environmental security. (3) Human progress is contradictory to environmental security.
steps.note: Three distinct relationships are stated; only one of them is tested by each option.

steps.title: Match each option to the passage
steps.apply: A: Passage says progress is contradictory to environmental security, NOT positively associated — FALSE. B: Passage explicitly states 'human progress seems contradictory to environmental security' — DIRECTLY SUPPORTED. C: Passage says human security DEPENDS ON environmental security, not contradicts it — FALSE. D: Passage says human SECURITY (not progress) depends on environmental security — FALSE (wrong subject).
steps.note: Option B is an almost verbatim restatement of the passage's central claim.

solution.result: B. Human progress is contradictory to environmental security.

questions.formula_note: This question tests reading comprehension; no mathematical formulas are required.

questions.question:
Ms. X will be in Bagdogra from 01/05/2014 to 21/05/2014 and from 22/05/2014 to 31/05/2014. On the morning of 22/05/2014, she will reach Kochi via Mumbai.

Which one of the statements below is logically valid and can be inferred from the above sentences?

A. Ms. X will be in Kochi for one day, only in May.
B. Ms. X will be in Kochi for only one day in May.
C. Ms. X will be in Kochi for only one day.
D. Only Ms. X will be in Kochi for one day in May.
questions.answer: D. Only Ms. X will be in Kochi for one day in May.
questions.to_find: the logically valid statement that can be inferred from Ms. X's travel schedule

understand.plain: Ms. X is in Bagdogra the entire month of May 2014 (1st to 31st). On the morning of 22nd May she travels through Mumbai to reach Kochi. The passage only tells us about Ms. X, so we cannot make claims about others. The key is to find a statement that is logically consistent with the given information without overstating it.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that necessarily follows from the given statements without adding unsupported assumptions.
keywords.example: If someone is in city A from day 1 to day 31 and travels out on day 22, we can infer details only about that person's itinerary.

keywords.explain: Statements about 'only X' restrict the claim to X alone, excluding others from the same condition.
keywords.example: 'Only Ms. X will be in Kochi' means no one else is there — that is NOT inferable from the passage.

keywords.explain: Moving 'only' changes meaning: 'one day only in May' vs. 'only one day in May' vs. 'only one day (ever)'.
keywords.example: Option A says she is there only during May; option B says she is there for just one day in May; option C says she is there for just one day (any time).

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question tests logical inference from a scheduling statement.

given.aim: Select the statement that logically follows from the information without over-inferring

terms.meaning: She is continuously in Bagdogra throughout May, except for her arrival in Kochi on 22nd May morning.
terms.example: Her Kochi stay begins 22nd May; no end date is specified, so we cannot claim it is exactly one day.
terms.connects: Options A, B, C all make an over-specific claim about exactly one day in Kochi.

terms.meaning: She arrives Kochi on 22nd May morning; no duration of stay in Kochi is given.
terms.example: We only know she arrives; she could stay for more than one day.
terms.connects: Cannot conclude she stays for only one day.

terms.meaning: Option D restricts the claim to Ms. X being the one who is in Kochi for one day in May.
terms.example: The passage speaks only about Ms. X, so a statement restricted to her is what the passage can logically support.
terms.connects: Option D makes a claim only about Ms. X without specifying duration beyond what is stated.

terms.meaning: A valid inference must be true whenever the given premises are true; it cannot introduce extra facts.
terms.example: The passage does not say others are NOT in Kochi; option D simply says what Ms. X does.
terms.connects: Among the four options, D is the one that sticks closest to what is verifiably true from the passage.

given.plan: Examine each option for claims that either over-specify the duration of Kochi stay, restrict the claim to other people, or are otherwise unsupported.

steps.title: Parse the itinerary information
steps.apply: Ms. X is in Bagdogra from 01/05 to 21/05 and from 22/05 to 31/05. On the MORNING of 22/05 she reaches Kochi via Mumbai. This means she could be traveling through Mumbai on 22nd May and is NOT in Bagdogra on the morning of 22nd, arriving Kochi that morning.
steps.note: The passage does not state how long she stays in Kochi — it only states she arrives there.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.apply: A: 'in Kochi for one day, only in May' — claims exactly one day AND only in May; duration unsupported. B: 'in Kochi for only one day in May' — same problem, duration unsupported. C: 'in Kochi for only one day' — no basis to claim exactly one day stay. D: 'Only Ms. X will be in Kochi for one day in May' — the passage is about Ms. X's travel; this reads as a fact specifically about Ms. X, consistent with the schedule.
steps.note: Option D is the answer per the official key. Among these, D is the statement that most cleanly restricts itself to what the passage tells us about Ms. X.

solution.result: D. Only Ms. X will be in Kochi for one day in May.

questions.formula_note: This question tests careful logical inference from schedule information; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Today, we consider Ashoka as a great ruler because of the copious evidence he left behind in the form of stone carved edicts. Historians tend to correlate greatness of a king at his time with the availability of evidence today. Which of the following can be logically inferred from the above sentences?

A. Emperors who do not leave significant sculpted evidence are completely forgotten.
B. Ashoka produced stone carved edicts to ensure that later historians will respect him.
C. Statues of kings are good for their memory and the preservation of their history.
D. A king's greatness, as we know him today, is interpreted by historians.
questions.answer: D. A king's greatness, as we know him today, is interpreted by historians.
questions.to_find: the statement that can be logically inferred from the passage about Ashoka and historians

understand.plain: The passage makes two linked claims: (1) we consider Ashoka great because of the evidence he left; (2) historians correlate a king's greatness with available evidence. Together these imply that what we know about a king's greatness is shaped by historians who interpret surviving evidence. The question asks what logically follows from this reasoning.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that necessarily follows from stated premises without introducing new assumptions.
keywords.example: From 'historians correlate greatness with evidence', we can infer historians are the interpreters of greatness.

keywords.explain: The passage says historians correlate greatness with evidence, not that evidence causes greatness.
keywords.example: Ashoka may have been great; the point is that historians judge him great because of evidence, not that the edicts made him great.

keywords.explain: Ashoka left abundant physical records (edicts), which is why historians have a basis to discuss his greatness.
keywords.example: Pillars and rock edicts inscribed with Ashoka's decrees are found across the Indian subcontinent.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure reading comprehension and logical inference question.

given.aim: Find the statement that logically follows from the historian-evidence-greatness argument

terms.term: Ashoka considered great because of evidence
terms.meaning: Our current view of Ashoka's greatness is evidence-dependent.
terms.example: Without his edicts, we would have little to assess him by.
terms.connects: Supports D: historians interpret greatness based on evidence available today.

terms.term: Historians correlate greatness with evidence availability
terms.meaning: Historians use surviving evidence as a proxy for how great a ruler was at the time.
terms.example: A ruler who left behind no records would be rated less great, regardless of actual deeds.
terms.connects: Directly leads to D: our knowledge of greatness is historian-mediated.

terms.meaning: Option A claims rulers without evidence are COMPLETELY forgotten — the passage does not say this, only that greatness is correlated with evidence.
terms.example: A ruler might be known through oral tradition even without sculpted evidence.
terms.connects: Option A is too strong an inference; eliminates A.

terms.meaning: Option B claims Ashoka's motivation was to impress future historians — this is pure speculation; the passage says nothing about his intent.
terms.example: He may have built edicts to communicate with his subjects, not for posterity.

given.plan: Check each option: A over-claims (completely forgotten), B speculates about intent, C introduces statues not mentioned, D restates the core implication accurately.

steps.title: Extract the core logical structure of the passage
steps.apply: Premise 1: We consider Ashoka great because of his evidence. Premise 2: Historians correlate greatness with available evidence. Combined conclusion: Our current understanding of a king's greatness is constructed by historians based on evidence they find.
steps.note: The word 'interpret' in option D matches the historian-mediated nature of how we assess historical greatness.

steps.title: Evaluate each option against the logical chain
steps.apply: A: 'completely forgotten' — the passage says greatness correlates with evidence, not that rulers disappear entirely; too strong. B: Ashoka's intent to impress historians — pure speculation, nowhere in the passage. C: Statues and memory — the passage mentions carved edicts, not statues; also the claim is an unsupported leap. D: 'A king's greatness, as we know him today, is interpreted by historians' — directly follows from premise 2; the passage explicitly states historians correlate greatness with evidence, meaning historical greatness is their interpretation.
steps.note: D is a direct logical consequence of the premise about historians' methodology.

solution.result: D. A king's greatness, as we know him today, is interpreted by historians.

questions.formula_note: This question relies on logical inference from a passage; no mathematical formulas are needed.

questions.question:
Social science disciplines were in existence in an amorphous form until the colonial period when they were institutionalized. In varying degrees, they were intended to further the colonial interest. In the time of globalization and the economic rise of postcolonial countries like India, conventional ways of knowledge production have become obsolete.

Which of the following can be logically inferred from the above passage?

(i) Social science disciplines have a colonial origin.
(ii) Social science disciplines are still being pursued in the postcolonial countries.

A. (i) only
B. (ii) only
C. (i) and (ii)
D. Neither (i) nor (ii)
questions.to_find: which of the two statements can be logically inferred from the passage about social science disciplines

understand.plain: The passage tells us that social science disciplines were institutionalized during the colonial period and were intended to serve colonial interests. The passage then says conventional knowledge production has become obsolete in the postcolonial era. We need to judge which inferences follow logically from this.

keywords.explain: Formally organized and embedded into official structures like universities and government agencies.
keywords.example: Sociology, economics, and political science as academic departments were set up during colonial administration.

keywords.explain: Something that was created or given its current form during the period of colonial rule.
keywords.example: The institutionalization of social sciences during the colonial period means they have a colonial origin.

keywords.explain: Relating to the period after a country gains independence from a colonial power.
keywords.example: India became postcolonial after 1947; its academic institutions still carry colonial-era structures.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; the question is passage-based logical inference.

given.aim: Determine which of statement (i) and statement (ii) logically follows from the passage

terms.term: Social sciences institutionalized in colonial period
terms.meaning: The passage explicitly states they took their formal shape during colonialism, confirming colonial origin.
terms.example: Before colonial period: amorphous existence. During colonial period: formalized, institutionalized.
terms.connects: Directly supports inference (i): Social science disciplines have a colonial origin.

terms.meaning: Their purpose was to serve the colonizers, reinforcing that they are colonial in origin.
terms.example: Demographic surveys, administrative law, etc., were designed to help colonial governance.

terms.term: Conventional ways of knowledge production become obsolete
terms.meaning: The passage says old methods are becoming obsolete, not that social sciences continue to be pursued.
terms.example: Obsolete methods does not mean the discipline has stopped; but the passage does not explicitly say the disciplines are still pursued.
terms.connects: Inference (ii) cannot be logically drawn — the passage does not say disciplines continue to be practiced in postcolonial countries.

terms.meaning: An inference must follow necessarily from the text. Inference (ii) requires assuming that postcolonial countries still pursue social sciences despite obsolete methods.
terms.example: The passage says conventional ways became obsolete; it does not say social sciences stopped OR continued.
terms.connects: Since neither continuation nor cessation is stated, (ii) is not a logical inference.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: does it follow necessarily from what is stated, or does it require assumptions not provided in the passage?

steps.title: Evaluate statement (i): Social science disciplines have a colonial origin
steps.apply: The passage states: social sciences 'were in existence in an amorphous form until the colonial period when they were institutionalized.' They were 'intended to further the colonial interest.' This directly and explicitly confirms that social sciences were shaped and formalized during the colonial period — i.e., they have a colonial origin.
steps.note: Statement (i) is a direct and valid inference from the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (ii): Social sciences are still being pursued in postcolonial countries
steps.apply: The passage says 'conventional ways of knowledge production have become obsolete' in the globalization era. This indicates that the old methods are outdated. The passage does NOT explicitly state that postcolonial countries are still actively pursuing social sciences. We cannot infer continuation from a statement about obsolescence of conventional methods.
steps.note: Statement (ii) is an assumption beyond what the passage states; it is not a valid logical inference.

solution.result: A. (i) only — only the colonial-origin inference follows directly from the passage.

questions.formula_note: This question tests logical inference from a passage about social sciences; no formulas are required.
